Mas a diferença mais importante não é velocidade nem memória, é autonomia. Na Apollo, os astronautas precisavam monitorar manualmente cada sistema o tempo todo, verificar trajetória, checar pressão, calcular correções. A reentrada mais rápida que seres humanos já enfrentaram. Imagine you are floating in deep space, far from Earth, far from the Sun, far from any star. Suddenly, his spacesuit malfunctions.  What would happen to you?  If you watch science fiction movies, you already know the answer, right?  You would freeze instantly.  It would turn into a cosmic ice statue.  But what if I told you that Hollywood lied to you?  The scientific truth is much stranger and, to be honest, much more terrifying.  The universe is a place of absurd extremes.  It has nuclear furnaces that burn at millions of degrees and dark voids where heat simply ceases to exist.  But just how cold is outer space, where is the coldest and most bizarre place in the entire universe located ?  Because space can be the worst possible place to try and cool something down.  To understand the cold of space, we need to forget everything we know about the cold here on Earth.  Think about it.  When you open the freezer door and feel that icy air on your face, what are you really feeling?  In physics, temperature is not a thing, it is a measure of motion.  It is the speed at which atoms and molecules are vibrating.  If they move quickly, the object is hot.  If they move slowly, the music is cold.  Simple as that.  But here we encounter our first major paradox.  Outer space is, for the most part, a vacuum, a vast, silent nothingness.  So, if temperature is the movement of particles, and there are no particles in a vacuum, what exactly happens?  Space has no temperature; it is neither hot nor cold; it simply is nothing.  Only matter can have a temperature.  So, when we ask how cold space is, what we're really asking is something else.  We're asking, if I threw a thermometer into the middle of nowhere, what temperature would it register?  Let's do this thought experiment.  Imagine you've traveled to deep space, far beyond any star.  You drop a thermometer in there.  It would begin to lose heat to the void.  Slowly the temperature would drop, and drop.  But guess what?  She wouldn't go down there forever. It would stop at a very specific number, 2,725 Kelvin, or, for us, an impressive -270ºC .  That's just 2.7 degrees above absolute zero.  The theoretical limit where all atomic motion stops completely .  But wait, why the thermometer for I?  Why doesn't he descend to absolute zero?  What is heating up deep space, even in the darkest and emptiest places in the universe?  The answer is a ghostly echo, the echo of the most violent event in history.  13.8 billion years ago, the universe wasn't cold; it was quite the opposite.  Immediately after the Big Bang, the cosmos was a soup of unimaginably hot plasma.  But the universe expanded and cooled.  About 400,000 years later, the first atoms were formed.  And it was at that exact moment that something incredible happened.  Light was able to travel freely through space for the first time.  A colossal burst of radiation flooded the cosmos.  This first light of the universe, it is still traveling.  She's walking past you right now .  But as the universe continued to expand, this light was stretched.  She lost energy.  What was once infernal heat is now just a faint whisper of microwaves. Scientists call this cosmic microwave background radiation, and it's what defines the baseline temperature of the universe.  No matter where you run, no matter how far you go to the stars, you will never escape this bath of primordial radiation.  That is why empty space never reaches absolute zero.  The echo of the Big Bang is still heating the cosmos, keeping everything at exactly -270°C .  But there's a fascinating detail.  As the universe continues to expand, this radiation is stretching even further.  Do you know what that means?  The universe is colder today than it has ever been.  And in the future it will get even colder, much colder than any place we know today.  Now that we know the temperature of the universe, let's return to our initial question.  If you were exposed to temperatures as low as -270 degrees Celsius, would you freeze like in the movies?  The answer will surprise you.  No. Actually, your biggest problem in space wouldn't be the cold, it would be the heat.  Like this?  To understand this, think about how things cool down here on Earth.  If you jump into a frozen lake, the icy water touches your skin and instantly steals your heat.  That's driving.  If you stand in the middle of a blizzard, the icy wind will carry your warmth away.  That's convention.  But [music] in space there is no water, no air, no wind, there is absolutely nothing to touch you and steal your warmth.  The vacuum of space is, in fact, the best thermal insulator that exists.  It's like the best thermos in the universe.  The only way you can lose heat in space is by radiating infrared waves.  And that takes time.  It takes a long time.  If you were ejected into space without a suit, you wouldn't freeze instantly.  His body would retain heat for hours.  Ironically, you would die of asphyxiation long before you even started to feel cold.  That's why designing spacecraft is a nightmare.  The problem for NASA isn't keeping the astronauts warm.  The problem is preventing them from cooking alive, without air to dissipate the heat. Computers and even the heat from the human body accumulate quickly.  The International Space Station needs gigantic radiators just to expel that heat.  And that brings us to another bizarre extreme.  Space isn't just cold; it's simultaneously the hottest and coldest place you can imagine.  But wait, if you don't freeze, what exactly happens to your body in a vacuum?  The reality is brutal.  Without the pressure of the earth pushing against you, the gases inside your body begin to expand, and rapidly.  If you tried to hold your breath, the air in your lungs would expand so much that it would tear the lung tissue.  The number one rule in space is: exhale all the air immediately. Furthermore, since the pressure in space is almost zero, the boiling point of liquids drops drastically.  The water in your body doesn't need heat to boil.  The lack of pressure does that for her.  The saliva on your tongue, the tears in your eyes, and the moisture in your lungs would begin to boil and evaporate instantly. His body would swell to twice its size.  But don't worry, you wouldn't explode like a balloon.  Your skin is elastic enough to hold anything in.  Another Hollywood myth debunked.  And we don't know this just from theory.  We know because a human being has already gone through this and survived.  In 1966, a NASA technician named Jim Leblanc was testing a spacesuit in a giant vacuum chamber.  Suddenly, a hose came loose.  In just 10 seconds, the pressure around Jim dropped to almost zero.  Do you know what the last thing he felt before he passed out was? Saliva boiling and bubbling on the tongue itself.  Fortunately, the team saved Jim in time.  He proved that a human has about 10 to 15 seconds of consciousness in a vacuum before blacking out. You don't turn into a cosmic popsicle, you simply switch off.  But if empty space is like this, what happens when we get close to a star, or worse, when we search for the coldest place in the entire universe?  While empty space rests at a tranquil -270º, all you have to do is get close to a star for the rules to change completely. Remember that space has no temperature, only matter does?  Because [music] is, the matter around our Sun is in a state of constant fury.  The surface of the Sun burns at about 5,500ºC. But here's one of the greatest mysteries of astrophysics.  The Sun's outer atmosphere, the Corona, is inexplicably hotter.  It reaches mind-blowing [music] speeds of 1 to 2 million degrees.  And you don't need to go all the way to the sun to experience these extremes.  Right here in Earth's orbit, things are brutal.  Imagine you are outside the International Space Station .  The part of your outfit that's facing the sun will bake at 121ºC. Hot enough to boil water, but the part of her outfit that's in the shade, she plunges to -157 degrees.  You would be baking and freezing at the same time, separated by only a few centimeters of fabric. As we move away from the sun, the cold sets in.  On Uranus, temperatures drop to -224ºC. On Pluto, ice is as hard as steel, at -240º. But do you want to know something surprising?  The coldest place in our solar system is n't out in the dark depths of Pluto.  He is much closer to home.  In the deep craters of our own moon's south pole, sunlight hasn't reached for billions of years.  There, scientists recorded temperatures of -23ºC. Yes, it's colder than the surface of Pluto, but even that lunar cold is nothing compared to what we find in deep space.  To find the true master of cosmic cold, we need to travel 5000 light-years away.  I present to you the boomerang nebula.  This is officially the coldest known natural place in the entire universe.  The average temperature at the heart of this nebula is 1 Kelvin.  That's -272.15ºC. Just one degree above absolute zero.  Think about how insane that is.  The boomerang nebula is colder than the empty space around it.  She is colder than the echo of the Big Bang.  It literally absorbs the heat of the universe because it is colder than it.  But how is that possible?  How can anything in nature be colder than empty space itself?  For decades, this was a complete mystery.  More recently, astronomers finally discovered the culprit, and the answer involves a stellar murder.  At the center of the boomerang nebula lies a dying red giant star.  But she wasn't dying alone.  A smaller companion star plunged directly into the heart of this giant.  This cosmic collision was an explosive trigger.  It ejected matter from the star into space at terrifying speeds, over 150 km/s, and this is where basic physics comes in.  When a gas expands too quickly, it cools down. It's the same principle as the cold gas that comes out of your aerosol deodorant, only on a galactic scale.  The expansion of gas in the boomerang nebula is so violent that the temperature has plummeted to near absolute zero.  It's a cosmic freezer created by the death of two stars.  But in astronomical terms, this is just a phase.  In a few thousand years, the expansion will slow down and the nebula will begin to heat up again.  But what if I told you that the coldest place in the universe isn't in space?  The boomerang nebula is the coldest natural place in the universe, but the key word here is natural.  Because if you want to find the truly coldest place in the entire cosmos, you need to look at Earth.  We humans have managed to create temperatures that make the boomerang nebula look like a summer day at the beach.  When we cool matter to near absolute zero, the normal laws of physics simply break down.  We've entered the bizarre realm of quantum mechanics.  If you cool helium gas to about 2 degrees above absolute zero, it turns into a superfluid.  It loses all its viscosity.  It flows without any friction, passes through microscopic holes, and even defies gravity, climbing the walls of a glass and trickling out.  But the scientists wanted to go further.  In 2018, NASA sent a laboratory to the International Space Station.  There, they managed to cool atoms to less than 1 billion degrees above absolute zero.  At this temperature, the atoms cease to act as individual particles.  They merge into a single giant quantum wave.  But the absolute record for cold was broken in 2021 in Germany, using a 122 m free-fall tower.  Scientists have managed to cool a gas to an unbelievable 38 picvins.  That's 38 trillionths of a degree above absolute zero.  For two brief seconds, as that capsule plummeted down the tower, that tiny cluster of atoms was the coldest point in the entire known universe. Colder than deep space, colder than the boomerang nebula we created on a small rocky planet.  But this raises an obvious question.  If we've come this close, why don't we just reach absolute zero once and for all?  Why don't we take the final step?  The answer lies embedded in the fundamental laws of our universe.  The third law of thermodynamics states that it is physically impossible to cool anything down to absolute zero.  Think of it this way: to cool something down, you need to transfer the heat from that object to something even colder. But how do you cool something down that's already at a lower temperature?  possible. [music] Furthermore, quantum mechanics tells us that particles never come to a complete stop.  Even at absolute zero, they always maintain a slight tremor called zero-point energy. Absolute zero is not just a number on a thermometer; it's an unattainable mathematical limit .  It is the temperature event horizon .  You can get infinitely close, but the universe will never let you touch it. Outer space is a place of absolute contradictions.  It has no temperature, but it dictates the temperature of everything that dares to enter it.  It has stellar furnaces that burn at millions of degrees, surrounded by a vacuum of -270 degrees Celsius.  It's an environment that would make your blood boil as your body slowly radiates heat into the infinite darkness.  But in a way, the cold of space is a comforting reminder of where we came from.  That slight warmth of 2.7º that permeates the vacuum is the last gasp of the Big Bang.   Slowly fading away over billions of years.  The universe is cooling down.  With each passing second, the cosmos gets an infinitesimal fraction colder.  In an unimaginably distant future, when the last star burns through all its fuel and fades away, the universe will finally plunge into total darkness.  The temperature will continue to fall, getting ever closer to that unattainable limit of absolute zero.  The universe will not end in fire; it will end in a cold, still, and eternal silence.  But until that day comes, we remain here in our little oasis of warmth, orbiting a yellow star, marveling at the beautiful and terrifying icy vastness that surrounds us.
